MILK ROAD · MACRO DASHBOARD v2 2026-05-15 · built 2026-05-15 20:30 UTC
Milk Road Macro Index · 2026-05-15
+0.73
STAY LONG
100% position
−3 0 · threshold +5
CASH ↓ ↑ LONG
What's behind it
Market signal (MMI) GREEN +0.23
Macro stress OFF
MRMI = MMI + macro buffer. Cash only when both turn against you.
This week’s read · 2026-05-12 (cached)

MRMI sits at +0.894 — comfortably LONG — but the signal's composition deserves scrutiny: essentially all of the margin above the zero line is supplied by the macro buffer running at full strength, while MMI at +0.394 is doing the minimum required and trending lower on the week. The same catalyst — Monday's 90-day US-China tariff truce — is simultaneously the source of GII's strength and the reason the economy pillar hasn't yet deteriorated enough to compress the buffer: the détente provided a temporary ceiling on tariff pass-through expectations, keeping the six-month core CPI drift in negative territory even as consensus pencils in a tick up in the April print, which means the inflation axis of the AND-gate hasn't flipped. The trouble is that both the market signal and the macro insulation hinge on the same 90-day clock: Dallas Fed research suggests tariff effects on PCE prices peaked in Q1, but Goldman's estimated one percentage point of incremental inflation from tariff pass-through hasn't fully cleared the pipeline, and if core CPI's six-month drift reverses into positive territory over the next two to three months — concurrent with the labor market softening past the −1σ threshold our real economy score is currently approaching — the buffer compresses rapidly. On the market side, breadth's -0.655 weekly move tells you the rally has already priced the truce without broad cyclical confirmation; the S&P's 14% surge in 23 sessions is a sentiment event, not a participation event, and MMI won't sustainably advance until semis, transports, homebuilders, and regional banks re-engage rather than fade. The two pillars are therefore reading the same macro moment from opposite angles: markets are celebrating a trade ceasefire while the economy quietly absorbs the lagged costs of the tariff regime that preceded it. Stay LONG — the buffer is doing exactly what it's designed to do — but the asymmetric risk is that the 90-day truce resolves the market's uncertainty without resolving the inflation pipeline, leaving the framework exposed to a stress trigger just as MMI breadth needs to carry more weight.

1How the index has evolved

Milk Road Macro Index (MRMI)

How it's built: MRMI combines MMI (market momentum from credit, breadth and volatility) with a macro buffer that erodes when growth and inflation both deteriorate. The buffer keeps us invested by default; only when the market signal turns red and macro stress builds does MRMI cross below zero and trigger CASH.

Reading the chart: white line is the MRMI value over time. Green shading = LONG regime, red = CASH. Notice the cash episodes around 2018 (vol spike), 2020 (COVID), and 2022 (bear market) — that's when both pillars confirmed danger. Toggle assets in the legend to overlay SPX / Russell / BTC.

Next: open MMI Drivers below to see what's behind the market signal, then Macro Backdrop for the economy signal.

A single regime signal: above zero stay long, below zero move to cash. Built from market momentum plus a macro stress buffer.

MRMI (headline) MMI (momentum only) S&P 500 Russell Bitcoin
How well does this work historically? (click)

10-year backtest (2016–2026), no leverage, vs buy-and-hold:

  • SPX +2.8% annual alpha · drawdown cut from −33.9% to −12.3%
  • Russell 2000 +6.0% annual alpha · drawdown cut from −41.1% to −25.2%
  • Bitcoin +7.0% annual alpha · drawdown cut from −83.4% to −70.2%

Active ~22% of the time (cash during stress). OOS (2023–2026, no major stress events): SPX flat, Russell +2.5%, BTC −26% — the cost of de-risking during a bull market without stress events. The framework's value is conditional on stress periods occurring; in calm bull markets it behaves like buy-and-hold.

2What the markets are signalingMarket pillar

MMI — Market Momentum Index. The fast, market-derived half of MRMI: built entirely from price and volatility data — credit spreads, cyclical sector breadth, and financial-conditions volatility — equally weighted. Markets react quickly, so MMI catches turning points early; the trade-off is they can also flash false alarms, which is why MMI alone never triggers a CASH call. It only acts in concert with the slower economy pillar below.

Market Momentum Index (MMI)

What you're seeing: the MMI score over time, equally-weighted from GII (global growth momentum), Breadth (cyclical participation) and FinCon (financial conditions). Above zero = healthy momentum. Below zero = momentum is rolling over.

Drivers below: click any row to expand the underlying series. The MMI score is just the average of the three.

Market momentum on its own, averaged from credit spreads, sector breadth and financial-conditions volatility.

This week’s read · market pillar · 2026-05-12 (cached)

The defining tension in this week's MMI read is a sharp internal divergence: GII is the lone driver gaining ground on a seven-day basis (+0.165), while Breadth has given back more than half a point (-0.655) and FinCon has softened modestly, leaving the composite at +0.394 — still green, but on a clearly deteriorating weekly trajectory. The GII lift traces directly to Monday's US-China 90-day tariff truce struck in Geneva: copper surged to $10,296/tonne on the announcement, HY spreads compressed further toward multi-year tights, and the yield curve — now 48 bps positively sloped at the 10s-2s — reinforced the forward-risk bid across GII's components. Breadth's deterioration tells the other side of the story: the S&P 500 ripped roughly 14% in 23 sessions since the early-April lows, but fewer than 60% of its constituents were trading above their 200-day moving averages at the highs — a participation deficit our cyclical ETF basket (semis, transports, homebuilders, regional banks, retail) captured as those sectors cooled sharply after their tariff-relief spike. FinCon remains modestly positive but lost ground intraday as VIX at 18.90 still carries enough residual fear premium to drag the component; MOVE at 70.74 is constructively low, however, signaling that bond vol has disengaged from equity anxiety — a split that often precedes equity vol normalization. The net read: GII is providing real fundamental support from the trade détente, but MMI cannot sustain a move higher unless Breadth closes the gap — the rally is genuine, yet still narrow enough relative to its headline that cyclical sector participation, not the truce itself, is the variable to watch in the sessions ahead.

Sources: - U.S. and China to ease tariffs significantly for 90 days — CBS News - Copper Prices Surge to $10,296/Tonne as US-China Truce Sparks Market Rally — Carbon Credits - Small caps steal the spotlight as S&P 500 rally masks weak breadth — Investing.com - Trade truce, or just a pause? The fragile state of US-China relations — Capital Economics - Stock Market Today (May 5, 2026): Small-caps and tech lead — TheStreet

MMI DRIVERS · GII · Breadth · FinCon — click any row to expand
3What the economy is signalingEconomy pillar

Macro Stress. The slow, economy-derived half of MRMI: built entirely from real-economy data — consumer spending, jobs, income, GDP nowcast — and inflation trajectory. The economy moves slowly, so stress takes time to build, but when it does it's grounded in fundamentals rather than market noise. Stress fires only when growth is weak and inflation is rising — that AND condition is what filters out the false alarms the market pillar would otherwise produce on its own.

Macro Stress Pressure

What you're seeing: the smoothed AND-condition between weak growth and rising inflation, expressed as a z-score against the full historical pressure series. 0σ = a typical macro reading; +1σ = stress building above the historical norm (top ~16% of readings); +2σ = unusual; +3σ = extreme. Negative values mean conditions are actively benign vs history.

How it's built: sigmoid(−2·Real_Economy) × sigmoid(2·Inflation_Direction), then z-scored against its own history. Same AND logic as the model's hard-clipped stress_intensity (which still drives MRMI internally) but always informative and self-anchored to history.

Underlying components: expand the section below to see the two raw axes — Real Economy Score and Inflation Direction Δ6m — that feed this score.

Stress pressure z-scored against its own history. Above +1σ = stress building above norm; below −1σ = unusually calm.

This week’s read · economy pillar · 2026-05-12 (cached)

The macro stress pressure score is sitting at its historical zero-point — z-score essentially flat — signaling that the AND-condition between weak growth and rising inflation remains far from threatening, with the hard-clipped stress intensity at zero and the macro buffer feeding MRMI at full strength. The two underlying axes are each mildly adverse but not simultaneously enough to build pressure: the real economy score has slipped to roughly −0.9σ, consistent with a labor market the April payrolls report described as effectively "frozen" — 115,000 net adds well below trend, the unemployment rate unchanged at 4.3%, and wage growth at 3.6% annualized, shy of estimates — though the Atlanta Fed's Q2 GDPNow estimate of 3.7% is anchoring the growth sub-component and preventing a deeper slide into the danger zone. On the inflation axis, the six-month directional change in core CPI remains negative at roughly −0.4pp, meaning that despite today's April CPI release arriving with consensus expecting a tick up to 2.7% core YoY (from March's 2.6%), the trend that actually feeds the model — the six-month drift — has not reversed; energy spillovers from the Middle East conflict are inflating the headline print but have not yet meaningfully re-accelerated core. The AND-gate multiplicative structure means these two modestly adverse readings, when multiplied together, produce near-zero intensity — neither axis is sufficiently extreme to overwhelm the other. What would shift this materially: a confirmed pivot in the six-month core CPI direction into positive territory over the next two to three months — especially if the tariff pass-through Goldman estimates at roughly 1 percentage point of incremental inflation accelerates — running concurrently with the real economy score deepening below −1σ on continued labor market softening. Until those two axes turn adverse in tandem, the pressure z-score stays near norm and the buffer remains intact.

Sources: - Jobs report April 2026 | CNBC - April hiring beat expectations, but economists warn the labor market is 'frozen' | CNN Business - GDPNow - Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta - Economists expect April CPI report to show inflation on the rise | Marketplace - Consumer Price Index (CPI) for April 2026 is Projected to Rise 3.7% Year-Over-Year | FactSet - Inflation Set to Rise in 2026 as Tariff Costs Hit Consumers | Morningstar - What to Expect From the April CPI Report | Kiplinger

UNDERLYING COMPONENTS · Real Economy Score · Inflation Direction Δ6m — click to expand
Real Economy Score (z, left axis) Inflation Direction Δ6m (pp, right axis)

4Reference Library
IndicatorCategoryLatestNotes
US M2 Money SupplyUS M2 Money Supply (FRED M2SL), shown as YoY % growth. Cross-currency global M2 would also include ECB / BOJ / PBOC — US M2 is the dominant component. Liquidity +3.9% Monetary fuel for risk assets · expanding = tailwind
US M2 Money Supply (FRED M2SL), shown as YoY % growth. Cross-currency global M2 would also include ECB / BOJ / PBOC — US M2 is the dominant component.
ISM Manufacturing PMIISM Manufacturing PMI is no longer redistributed via FRED (ISM licensing). Could substitute Empire State or Philly Fed manufacturing surveys as proxies. Activity — Data pending — ISM doesn't redistribute via FRED
Atlanta Fed GDPNowAtlanta Fed GDPNow — real-time nowcast of current-quarter Real GDP growth (annualized %). Updates several times per quarter as new data prints. Activity +3.99% Closes the publication-lag gap on official GDP
Atlanta Fed GDPNow — real-time nowcast of current-quarter Real GDP growth (annualized %). Updates several times per quarter as new data prints.
CFNAIChicago Fed National Activity Index — composite of 85 monthly US activity indicators, normalized to mean 0. Above 0 = above-trend growth, below 0 = below-trend. Activity -0.20 Comprehensive cross-sector US activity composite
Chicago Fed National Activity Index — composite of 85 monthly US activity indicators, normalized to mean 0. Above 0 = above-trend growth, below 0 = below-trend.
Industrial ProductionIndustrial Production index (manufacturing + mining + utilities), shown as YoY % change. Long historical series, biased toward goods-producing sectors. Activity +1.5% Goods-side activity · partial overlap with copper
Industrial Production index (manufacturing + mining + utilities), shown as YoY % change. Long historical series, biased toward goods-producing sectors.
Housing StartsHousing Starts (thousands of units, SAAR). Rate-sensitive — tends to lead the cycle when the Fed pivots. Activity 2K Rate-sensitive · leads the cycle on Fed pivots
Housing Starts (thousands of units, SAAR). Rate-sensitive — tends to lead the cycle when the Fed pivots.
Building PermitsBuilding Permits (thousands of units, SAAR). Leads housing starts by 1–2 months — even more forward-looking on construction activity. Activity 1K Permits lead starts by 1–2 months
Building Permits (thousands of units, SAAR). Leads housing starts by 1–2 months — even more forward-looking on construction activity.
Initial Jobless ClaimsInitial unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Highest-frequency real-economy signal in the framework — recession indicator when the 4-week moving average rises sharply. Labor 211K Weekly · most reliable recession indicator
Initial unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Highest-frequency real-economy signal in the framework — recession indicator when the 4-week moving average rises sharply.
Continuing Jobless ClaimsContinuing unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Tracks how long workers stay unemployed — rising trend can signal labor-market deterioration even when initial claims are low. Labor 1,782K Companion to initial claims · tracks duration of unemployment
Continuing unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Tracks how long workers stay unemployed — rising trend can signal labor-market deterioration even when initial claims are low.
Library entries don't drive the binary call — they're context that explains narrative shifts. Most need data wiring before they can populate.
